item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't realize the implications until the end, that's really nice. +1

item.title: Technobabble FTW
item.content:encoded: This actually looks like an actual professional document. It's not terribly scary or interesting but hey, it is what it is and I can appreciate it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one, it's a little complex, but its specificity makes sense if you consider the implications of it.

Perhaps it created the ocean and unwittingly contributed to the formation of life on earth?

I like that such things are left to the reader's imagination, this is just a scientific concept with a lot of unexplained depth

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too many big word to descibe a chemical-changing ball of ice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP quite a lot. And since I like 4D shapes I managed to find this picture: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:600-cell.gif which seems pretty appropriate. It would help get the hypericosahedron shape across at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made me go "huh". Interesting retake on the original. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aah, I see. So "metastable" is more like "precariously stable". Thank you very much, Drewbear, VAElynx.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's not a really great example because metastable is wider than that. *puts engineer hat on*

For a practical example of a metastable state, take a PVC drinks bottle. When you put hot water in it, it shrinks and becomes opaque, right? That's because the transparent and comparably lower density polyethylene is a metastable state of denser, opaque, crystalline PE, and if you give the molecules a bit of a thermal kick so that they can actually get to move around, the polymer chains will rearrange themselves into the actually stable state.

In fact, a lot of these states (another example would be martensite produced by quenching steel) follow the same general rule of being an unstable state preserved by kinetics.

It's kinda like… say we have stable states. You at work, you at home and you at Costco, say, with you not moving much (aka, order of 10m displacement from initial point inside the building) while there because you got things to do/like being there.
Now, you end up in your car to go from work to home ,and suddenly, there's a traffic jam. Now, a silly outside observer could say that you're in a stable state because you aren't moving - the fact is that you're trying to go home, but can't, because everyone else who is also trying to go home and can't is blocking your path, originally caused by a snow drift. And, unless someone removes the snow drift (adds energy, in this case mechanical by plowing it away) … yeah, no, you aren't going anywhere.

Congratulation, the clogged highway is now a perfect analogy of a metastable state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a scientific/engineering sense, "metastable" does NOT mean "super-stable". It means that the whatever is currently stable, but very very easy to destabilize. For instance, if you managed to balance a sharpened pencil on its tip, it'd be metastable: perferctly fine and balanced so long as absolutely nothing touches it.1 In this case, ice XII is metastable in that it normally only forms at very specific heat/temperature combinations and outside of that it turns into a different form of ice/water. SCP-045 is able to remain in the form of ice XII within a massively increased range, which is part of its anomalous properties.

Footnotes
1. Edit: herp-derp, I got this wrong. See here, instead. Same basic idea applies, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the more subtle version of the "point" in this rewrite, that this thing seeded life on Earth. +1

Unrelated, I googled searched "Ice XII" and now I am just all sorts of confused. What exactly does "metastable ice" mean? In layman's terms. Is it ice that never melts or something? I find that hard to believe, so clearly I'm just understanding the science wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I agree. That's why I specifically noted the 600 pyramidal faces. But hypericosahedron really does sound cooler :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really appreciate the science fixes in this rewrite, but still it's not enough for me to upvote. Nuetral vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I really like all the technobabble, the science and pseudoscience behind this is pretty neato, but overall it doesn't really leave an impression on me. Though I'm glad it doesn't just generate stuff, but is actually shown to change molecular structures. That at least makes it stand out.

item.title: Staff Post: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: Just noting for the record here that this was an officially sanctioned rewrite, approved by djkaktus et all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are We Clinical Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Cool" is not clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. A literal sort of 'seed of life'. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But "hypericosahedron" sounds so much cooler!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1.

Technical note: The standard techincal term for a "hypericosahedron" is a 600-cell, so that is likely what the Foundation would use in documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well having read both just now, this is a really good rewrite. But holy crap some of that got very technical. Should have paid better attention in Chemistry during high school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what rewrites are made of. Same implication at the end as that of the original, but without the "FOR SCIENCE! lol" cartoonishness. I want to read more about this SCP, want to know more about it. The original? I just felt kinda meh. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much more subtly done than the original, and in general much better written, while still preserving the core concept of "alien terraformer responsible for earth's current state."

item.title: Re: Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
I tried to look into who originally wrote it when I was researching for my rewrite request, but I came up blank.

Glad to see this was finally completed, if a little bitter I didn't get to do it. ;)

As for the content, there's a paragraph on discovery that feels very tacked on. It also raises a question about how the hell they found this in the middle of the ocean (even with the seismic activity). Also obligatory death of Foundation staff.

I think I'm a novote right now, but given how much better this is than the original… I may change my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: made me go "what the fuck" several times while reading. According to my standards, that's an upvote

item.title: Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
This is a rewrite of the original "Atmospheric Converter", text below. Screenshots of the comment thread are attached to the article.

+ show block
– hide block

Item #: SCP-045

Object Class: Euclid

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-045 must be kept within Decompression Chamber 32-s(D) at all times. The atmosphere of Decompression Chamber 32-s(D) should be composed of inert gases (xenon and helium preferred). In the event SCP-045 is exposed to normal air, the entire containment sector will be sealed and flooded with helium, pending unlock order by overseer class personnel.

Any personnel entering the chamber should be equipped with full pressure suits with internal oxygen rebreathers, and should avoid contact with SCP-045.

Description: SCP-045 was found hibernating in an undersea hot current at a depth of ████ ████████, ████ ███████ and █████ (████) fathoms, at ████'█W ████'█N. It was recovered by a submarine expedition and brought on board a bathysphere. The bathysphere was found a week after submerging, floating some kilometers from its original location, and filled with water under incredible pressure. These circumstances alerted the Foundation, and the bathysphere was summarily retrieved.

SCP-045 is, in its hibernating state, a perfect icosahedron made of a granular material which correlates with no known spectroscopic data. Since it is usually found at high pressures, researchers speculate that SCP-045 takes its shape due to the compacting effect acting on it. SCP-045 reacts with atmospheric nitrogen to create large amounts of water. As the water created exceeds the mass of nitrogen available, it can be safely assumed that SCP-045 draws upon mass from extra-local sources.

SCP-045 generates spherical bands of water at least five centimeters from its surface, allowing it to continue drawing on atmospheric nitrogen. These bands form a meta-stable vortex that moves counter-clockwise at speeds directly proportional to the amount of water SCP-045 is immersed in. In simulations this vortex exists at depths of over three kilometers. Full submersion in water at pressures above three hundred and eighty (380) atmospheres (thirty eight thousand, five hundred (38500) kPA) causes the vortex to collapse, at which point SCP-045 enters a dormant state due to lack of nitrogen.

The composition of water generated by SCP-045 is similar to that of sea water, and is created at temperatures between ten to thirty two (10-32) Celsius.

Addendum #045-1: Analysis of the landing site of SCP-045 unearthed extra-terrestrial meteorite matter, lending credence to the theory that Earth did not acquire terrestrial water through geological means. Instead, when SCP-045 was introduced to the planet, it rapidly began generating water, eventually raising sea levels to current-day levels.

Computer models show that seawater generation peaked roughly in the Pre-Cambrian era, after which continental drift and water generation slowly drove SCP-045 to depths upwards of three thousand, eight hundred (3800) fathoms, sending it into hibernation.

Addendum #045-2: It has been suggested that a sudden earthquake in the Mediterranean may have raised SCP-045 briefly in the Age of Myth to a 'sweet spot', causing SCP-045 to generate a whirlpool well in excess of two hundred (200) meters across. This may explain the continual reference to Charybdis in myths of that time.

Memo regarding #045: "I'm telling you, find me a planet where atmospheric nitrogen is abundant and I'll get clearance to launch this baby there. Terraforming on the cheap!" — ███████ ██████████


Deletion of the original was done at -17 and witnessed by ProcyonLotor.

Unfortunately, I'm a fucking idiot and forgot to write down who the original author was. My deepest apologies.

Edit: Thanks to AndarielHalo, the person who posted the original version was xthevilecorruptor. This very likely means that it was ported by him over from the site's prior home at EditThis, however, so who knows who the actual original author was.
